subject: Civil Procedure
subtopic: Supplemental jurisdiction - claim by plaintiff against a Rule 14 party
secondPassNotes: Second pass clarified that the original action rests solely on diversity, specified the third-party defendant's citizenship and amount, and removed any possible federal-question basis.

subject: Civil Procedure
subtopic: Rule 12 defenses - waiver by omission from the first Rule 12 motion
secondPassNotes: Second pass made clear that the first motion preceded the answer and that the omitted defense was personal jurisdiction, one of the waivable Rule 12(b)(2)-(5) defenses.

subject: Civil Procedure
subtopic: Preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders - notice requirement
secondPassNotes: Second pass separated the relief requested from relief potentially available and included both Rule 65(b) prerequisites so the question does not turn on a missing TRO fact.

subject: Civil Procedure
subtopic: Discovery - physical examination under Rule 35
secondPassNotes: Second pass removed any dispute about whether the condition was in controversy and made the missing court order the single dispositive defect.

subject: Civil Procedure
subtopic: Final judgment rule - unresolved claims and Rule 54(b)
secondPassNotes: Second pass specified that the claims are separate but that no Rule 54(b) direction or no-just-reason finding was made.

subject: Constitutional Law
subtopic: State sovereign immunity - prospective relief against a state officer
secondPassNotes: Second pass limited the requested remedy to prospective injunctive relief and identified the officer charged with enforcement, avoiding ambiguity about retrospective treasury relief.
authorityNotes: Ex parte Young, 209 U.S. 123 (1908) | Edelman v. Jordan, 415 U.S. 651 (1974)

subject: Constitutional Law
subtopic: Congressional commerce power - aggregation of intrastate economic activity
secondPassNotes: Second pass described a comprehensive regulation of a fungible interstate commodity and avoided relying on noneconomic conduct, which would trigger a different Commerce Clause analysis.
authorityNotes: Wickard v. Filburn, 317 U.S. 111 (1942) | Gonzales v. Raich, 545 U.S. 1 (2005)

subject: Constitutional Law
subtopic: Presidential power - refusal to spend funds that Congress directed be disbursed
secondPassNotes: Second pass eliminated any statutory discretion and framed the measure as a completed appropriation to identified eligible recipients, making the Take Care issue dispositive.
authorityNotes: U.S. Const. art. II, § 3 (Take Care Clause) | Train v. City of New York, 420 U.S. 35 (1975) | Clinton v. City of New York, 524 U.S. 417 (1998)

subject: Constitutional Law
subtopic: Equal protection - state alienage classification and political-function exception
secondPassNotes: Second pass selected a licensed clinical position with no policy-making authority, avoiding uncertainty about jobs such as police officers, teachers, or notaries that have generated political-function litigation.
authorityNotes: Graham v. Richardson, 403 U.S. 365 (1971) | Sugarman v. Dougall, 413 U.S. 634 (1973) | Bernal v. Fainter, 467 U.S. 216 (1984)

subject: Constitutional Law
subtopic: Freedom of expression - licensing scheme with unbridled discretion
secondPassNotes: Second pass added the lack of deadlines and prompt review while preserving unbridled discretion as the central defect; it also made the facial-challenge posture explicit.
authorityNotes: Shuttlesworth v. City of Birmingham, 394 U.S. 147 (1969) | City of Lakewood v. Plain Dealer Publishing Co., 486 U.S. 750 (1988)

subject: Contracts
subtopic: UCC acceptance by shipment - nonconforming goods sent as an accommodation
secondPassNotes: Second pass placed the accommodation notice with the shipment, made it conspicuous and explicit, and avoided facts suggesting a prior contract independent of the purchase order.

subject: Contracts
subtopic: Statute of frauds - possibility of performance within one year
secondPassNotes: Second pass stated that completion within 10 months was physically possible and that the contract imposed no minimum term, removing uncertainty about theoretical versus contractual possibility.

subject: Contracts
subtopic: Assignment of rights - effect of a prohibition that does not make assignments void
secondPassNotes: Second pass limited the assignment to an accrued monetary right, specified that the clause did not use voiding language, and established notice before payment.

subject: Contracts
subtopic: Liquidated damages - reasonable forecast of difficult-to-measure loss
secondPassNotes: Second pass narrowed the gap between stipulated and actual loss and expressly established the reasonableness of the contemporaneous estimate, reducing penalty ambiguity.
authorityNotes: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356 | U.C.C. § 2-718(1) (analogous sales rule)

subject: Contracts
subtopic: Third-party beneficiaries - vesting and modification
secondPassNotes: Second pass expressly negated each recognized vesting event and placed rescission before the daughter learned of the agreement.

subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
subtopic: Embezzlement - conversion of property lawfully possessed
secondPassNotes: Second pass used an independent financial manager with a segregated client account, fixed the intent after lawful possession, and stated that the jurisdiction uses common-law offense definitions.

subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
subtopic: Self-defense - initial aggressor who withdraws and communicates withdrawal
secondPassNotes: Second pass added an explicit verbal and physical withdrawal, an imminent deadly threat, and no safe retreat so the result does not depend on jurisdictional retreat rules.

subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
subtopic: Conspiracy - withdrawal after the agreement but before substantive offenses
secondPassNotes: Second pass expressly supplied the jurisdiction's no-overt-act rule, placed withdrawal before the burglary, and included both communication and police notification.
authorityNotes: Common-law conspiracy doctrine | Pinkerton v. United States, 328 U.S. 640 (1946)

subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
subtopic: Fourth Amendment - consent by a co-occupant with common authority
secondPassNotes: Second pass confined the search to undeniably shared areas and stated that the absent occupant had not made a contemporaneous objection.
authorityNotes: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. 164 (1974) | Georgia v. Randolph, 547 U.S. 103 (2006) | Fernandez v. California, 571 U.S. 292 (2014)

subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
subtopic: Miranda - questioning by an undercover agent
secondPassNotes: Second pass specified that no robbery charge had been filed and that the statement was voluntary, eliminating separate Sixth Amendment and due-process grounds for exclusion.

subject: Evidence
category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
subtopic: Present sense impression - statement describing an event while observing it
secondPassNotes: Second pass stipulated authentication and contemporaneous transmission so admissibility turns only on the hearsay exception.

subject: Evidence
category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
subtopic: Former testimony - unavailable declarant and prior opportunity with similar motive
secondPassNotes: Second pass made the deposition a trial-preservation deposition prompted by terminal illness and included full cross-examination on the identical traffic-signal issue, making similar motive especially clear.

subject: Evidence
subtopic: Liability insurance - admissible to show witness bias
secondPassNotes: Second pass framed the evidence exclusively as employment-based bias and removed facts that might invite an improper inference of negligence.
authorityNotes: Fed. R. Evid. 411 | Fed. R. Evid. 403

subject: Evidence
subtopic: Original-writing rule - admissibility of a duplicate
secondPassNotes: Second pass stipulated accuracy, removed authenticity and fairness concerns, and made the objection rest solely on the absence of the original.

subject: Evidence
category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
subtopic: Prior identification - declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination
secondPassNotes: Second pass stated that the photo procedure was proper, kept the declarant on the stand, and used memory loss rather than total refusal to testify.
authorityNotes: Fed. R. Evid. 801(d)(1)(C) | United States v. Owens, 484 U.S. 554 (1988)

subject: Real Property
subtopic: Landlord-tenant - duty to mitigate after abandonment
secondPassNotes: Second pass expressly adopted the modern majority rule, negated acceptance of surrender, and avoided stating how quickly this unit necessarily would have relet, so the answer properly describes the recoverable measure.
authorityNotes: Modern majority landlord-tenant mitigation rule | Sommer v. Kridel, 378 A.2d 767 (N.J. 1977)

subject: Real Property
subtopic: Easements - use to benefit after-acquired land
secondPassNotes: Second pass specified an appurtenant easement, identified the original dominant parcel, and stated that Parcel C was acquired later, making after-acquired-land misuse the sole issue.
authorityNotes: Common-law easement scope doctrine | Restatement (Third) of Property: Servitudes § 4.11

subject: Real Property
subtopic: Statute of frauds - part performance of an oral land-sale contract
secondPassNotes: Second pass stated the jurisdiction's doctrine and included all three classic corroborating acts, avoiding state-to-state uncertainty about whether any single act suffices.
authorityNotes: Traditional equitable part-performance doctrine | Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 129

subject: Real Property
subtopic: Transfer by mortgagee - mortgage follows the transferred debt
secondPassNotes: Second pass removed negotiability and recording complications and expressly stated that the note transfer was valid and no recording-act issue exists.
authorityNotes: Carpenter v. Longan, 83 U.S. (16 Wall.) 271 (1872) | Common-law mortgage-transfer doctrine

subject: Real Property
subtopic: General warranty deed - present covenant of seisin
secondPassNotes: Second pass made the neighbor's superior ownership undisputed and specified no disturbance, isolating seisin from the future covenants.

subject: Torts
subtopic: False imprisonment - intentional confinement without reasonable means of escape
secondPassNotes: Second pass established awareness, a locked sole exit, lack of consent, and lack of reasonable grounds, removing disputes about escape or shopkeeper privilege.

subject: Torts
subtopic: Necessity - private necessity and liability for actual damage
secondPassNotes: Second pass made the emergency severe, the entry necessary, and the owner careful, ensuring that liability rests on the incomplete privilege rather than negligence.
authorityNotes: Restatement (Second) of Torts §§ 197, 263 | Vincent v. Lake Erie Transportation Co., 124 N.W. 221 (Minn. 1910)

subject: Torts
subtopic: Negligence per se - protected class and type of harm
secondPassNotes: Second pass matched the protected class and harm precisely, expressly negated excuse, and preserved causation and damages as separate elements.

subject: Torts
subtopic: Vicarious liability - minor detour within the scope of employment
secondPassNotes: Second pass supplied distance, duration, return-to-route facts, and no contrary employer rule, making the detour characterization strong without eliminating realistic distractors.

subject: Torts
subtopic: Private nuisance - unusually sensitive use of land
secondPassNotes: Second pass separated the hypersensitivity issue from regulatory compliance and stated that ordinary homes and activities were unaffected.
